title: Where and What Questions: Here, There, and Simple Location Help
objective: Use อะไร and ที่ไหน with อยู่ to ask what something is, ask where something is, and understand short location answers such as here and there.

teachingFrame.openingHook: Open on a new learner holding a map at a school desk and not knowing what the object is or where to go next.
teachingFrame.scenario: A beginner arrives at a small school or community centre, points to an object, asks what it is, and then asks where the bathroom and room 103 are.
teachingFrame.learnerTakeaway: By the end, the learner can ask what this/that is, ask where a place is, and handle a short help-seeking exchange using here/there answers.

knownVocabulary.thai: สวัสดีครับ
คุณชื่ออะไร
มาจากอังกฤษ
มาจากอเมริกา
คุณคือ...ใช่ไหม
คนนี้คือใคร
เขามาจากไทย

knownVocabulary.notes: Teach the careful full form first.
Useful for room numbers, phone numbers, and other labelled numbers.

knownVocabulary.thai: ฉันอายุสิบปี
เขาอายุเก้าปี
knownVocabulary.english: he / she is nine years old

knownGrammar: Combine the greeting from lesson 1 with the new identity patterns so the learner can produce a practical mini-introduction.
Combine the new who-question pattern with earlier name and country material so the learner can follow and produce a short first-meeting dialogue.
Complete the 0-10 set and introduce เบอร์อะไร as a practical chunk for asking about room numbers, table numbers, and other everyday number labels.
Explain how คือ works in beginner identity lines so the learner can introduce someone or identify what is in front of them without treating it as a rigid English equals sign.
Explain the beginner-level pronoun choice so the learner can produce self-introduction lines without freezing over identity wording.
Explain what ครับ, ค่ะ, and คะ do so the learner does not memorize them as random decoration.
Give the learner a clean starting map for zero through three and explain that everyday Thai codes are usually said digit by digit.
Give the learner a small response toolkit so a first encounter can continue instead of stopping at one memorised line.
Move from isolated digits into usable age and room-number phrases while explicitly reusing the pronoun and identity material from the rebuilt foundation lessons.
Teach pronouns as tools for basic interaction, not as abstract dictionary entries, so the learner can point to self and others naturally.
Teach the core greeting and show that politeness is built into the line, not added as an afterthought.
Teach the core name pattern with ชื่อ and อะไร so the learner can ask and answer the most basic identity question immediately.
Teach the identity question with ใคร so the learner can ask who a person is in a short natural exchange and not confuse it with the earlier name question.
Teach the middle set where beginners often blur vowels or rush final consonants, especially with สี่, ห้า, หก, and เจ็ด.
Teach the origin pattern so the learner can complete a basic introduction with country information instead of stopping after the name.
Teach the two highest-value courtesy phrases and explain that one form repairs the interaction while the other closes it warmly.

sample.notes: Teach the careful full form first.

sample.thai: คุณชื่ออะไร

sections.heading: Ask what something is with อะไร
sections.purpose: Teach อะไร as the beginner what-question and connect it to the pointing words นี่ and นั่น in fixed usable chunks.
spokenNarration: Start with the question word อะไร à-rai, which means what.
Now plug it into two very useful pointer chunks: นี่อะไร nîi à-rai, what is this, and นั่นอะไร nân à-rai, what is that.
The important beginner habit is that Thai often leaves the question word near the end, so learn the whole chunk instead of forcing English word order into it.
onScreenBullets: อะไร | à-rai | what
นี่ | nîi | this
นั่น | nân | that
นี่อะไร | nîi à-rai | what is this?
นั่นอะไร | nân à-rai | what is that?
drills: Pointing drill: teacher points to a near object or far object, learner asks นี่อะไร or นั่นอะไร.
Chunk drill: learner hears อะไร and chooses whether to start with นี่ or นั่น based on distance.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show near versus far pointing clearly and make the question-word-at-the-end pattern easy to notice.
teachingVisuals: Two large cards labelled นี่ and นั่น with a near/far visual cue.
A highlighted underline on อะไร at the end of both question chunks.
teacherCues: Gesture near and far physically while saying นี่ and นั่น.
Pause on อะไร so the learner notices the question word position.

imageSupport.rationale: This section teaches a chunk pattern and pointing contrast, so typography and distance cues are clearer than a real-world image.

sections.heading: Ask where something is with ที่ไหน and อยู่
sections.purpose: Teach ที่ไหน as where and show how อยู่ carries the practical meaning of be located or live depending on what comes before it.
spokenNarration: Now build the where-question with ที่ไหน thîi-nǎi, where, and อยู่ yùu, which often means be located or live.
With a place like ห้องน้ำ, อยู่ usually means is located, so ห้องน้ำอยู่ที่ไหน asks where the bathroom is.
For short answers, do not build full sentences first. Learn the tight beginner replies อยู่ที่นี่, it is here, and อยู่ที่นั่น, it is there.
onScreenBullets: ที่ไหน | thîi-nǎi | where
อยู่ | yùu | be located / live
ที่นี่ | thîi-nîi | here
ที่นั่น | thîi-nân | there
ห้องน้ำอยู่ที่ไหน | hâawng-náam yùu thîi-nǎi | where is the bathroom?
อยู่ที่นี่ / อยู่ที่นั่น | yùu thîi-nîi / yùu thîi-nân | it is here / there
drills: Question-answer drill: teacher asks ห้องน้ำอยู่ที่ไหน, learner answers with อยู่ที่นี่ or อยู่ที่นั่น based on the visual cue.
Meaning drill: learner hears อยู่ and decides whether the chunk is about location or a person living/staying.

languageFocus.thai: ห้องน้ำอยู่ที่ไหน
คุณอยู่ที่ไหน
languageFocus.english: where do you live / where are you?
languageFocus.notes: With a person, อยู่ can mean live, stay, or be depending on context.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Make the location question concrete with a simple sign and then contrast here versus there as short answer patterns.
teachingVisuals: Large question card for ห้องน้ำอยู่ที่ไหน with a bathroom-sign anchor image kept behind the left panel only.
Two answer cards underneath: อยู่ที่นี่ and อยู่ที่นั่น with simple arrow cues.
teacherCues: Explain อยู่ once in plain language, then return to chunks quickly.
Keep the answer cards visually lighter than the main question so the learner sees the pattern first.

imageSupport.rationale: A single wayfinding or bathroom-sign image makes the location question feel real and helps the learner connect the chunk to an everyday service situation.

sections.heading: Recycle people words and numbers inside location questions
sections.purpose: Show that the new question system can already combine with review pronouns and digits, especially in simple room-location and person-location lines.
spokenNarration: You already know the review word คุณ from earlier chunks, and you already know เขา, so now the new question frame immediately becomes more useful.
คุณอยู่ที่ไหน can ask where you live or where you are, and เขาอยู่ที่นี่ is a short answer about a person being here.
Now bring back the digits from the last lesson and ask ห้องหนึ่ง-ศูนย์-สามอยู่ที่ไหน. The number is no longer isolated. It is part of a real location question.
onScreenBullets: คุณอยู่ที่ไหน | khun yùu thîi-nǎi | where do you live / where are you?
เขาอยู่ที่นี่ | khǎo yùu thîi-nîi | he / she is here
ห้องหนึ่ง-ศูนย์-สามอยู่ที่ไหน | hâawng nùeng-sǔun-sǎam yùu thîi-nǎi | where is room one-zero-three?
อยู่ที่นั่น | yùu thîi-nân | it is there
drills: Swap drill: teacher changes the subject from คุณ to เขา to ห้องหนึ่ง-ศูนย์-สาม, learner keeps the location pattern stable.
Review drill: learner hears 1-0-3 and says the room question slowly with the digits spoken one by one.

languageFocus.thai: เขาอยู่ที่นี่
ห้องหนึ่ง-ศูนย์-สามอยู่ที่ไหน

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show how the same where-question frame works with a person and with a room number, while reinforcing the last lesson’s digits.
teachingVisuals: Stacked prompt cards for person question versus room-number question.
A small 1-0-3 review strip beside the room question to reinforce digit-by-digit reading.
teacherCues: Say the room digits carefully one by one.
Keep the review strip small so the new question frame stays visually dominant.

imageSupport.rationale: This section is about combining review vocabulary into a stable pattern, so text-led drills are more useful than another photo.

sections.heading: Build a small help-seeking exchange
sections.purpose: Turn the new what and where chunks into a coherent first-contact service interaction that reuses courtesy language and confirmation.
spokenNarration: A real beginner does not need a long conversation yet. A short, polite help-seeking exchange is enough.
Start with ขอโทษ to get attention, ask นี่อะไร if you are pointing at an object, then ask ห้องน้ำอยู่ที่ไหน if you need a place.
One useful review link is that the answer can reuse the older identity frame นี่คือ..., while the new question is what changes.
Finally, confirm the room with ใช่ไหม if needed, and close politely with ขอบคุณ and ไม่เป็นไร.
onScreenBullets: ขอโทษค่ะ | khǎaw-thôot khâ | excuse me
นี่อะไรคะ | nîi à-rai khá | what is this?
นี่คือแผนที่ครับ | nîi kheuu phǎaen-thîi khráp | this is a map
ห้องน้ำอยู่ที่ไหนคะ | hâawng-náam yùu thîi-nǎi khá | where is the bathroom?
ห้องหนึ่ง-ศูนย์-สามอยู่ที่นี่ใช่ไหมคะ | hâawng nùeng-sǔun-sǎam yùu thîi-nîi châi mái khá | room one-zero-three is here, right?
drills: Role swap drill: one learner is the receptionist, one is the new student; switch roles after one full exchange.
Prompt drill: teacher shows object, bathroom sign, or room 103; learner chooses the correct question chunk.

languageFocus.thai: นี่คือแผนที่ครับ
ห้องน้ำอยู่ที่ไหนคะ
อยู่ที่นั่นครับ
ห้องหนึ่ง-ศูนย์-สามอยู่ที่นี่ใช่ไหมคะ
languageFocus.translit: hâawng nùeng-sǔun-sǎam yùu thîi-nîi châi mái khá

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Turn the lesson into a readable service-desk exchange with one object question, one location question, and one room confirmation.
teachingVisuals: Dialogue ladder showing the map question, bathroom question, and room confirmation as separate beats.
A small map or reception counter anchor kept behind the left panel only.
teacherCues: Keep each question on screen alone before showing the answer.
Do not let the map anchor compete with the Thai text or spill into the right-third camera zone.

imageSupport.rationale: A small reception-desk or map visual helps the learner understand why both a what-question and a where-question appear in the same exchange.
searchQueries: simple reception desk map brochure close up

roleplay.scenario: New student asking for help at a small school reception desk.

recap: Use อะไร at the end of a short chunk to ask what something is.
Use ที่ไหน with อยู่ to ask where something is.
Learn อยู่ที่นี่ and อยู่ที่นั่น as quick beginner location answers.
Recycle คุณ, เขา, and the digits from the last lesson so the new questions stay practical.
